cluster.title: Scrabble & Word Game Help — Kefiw
cluster.subhead: Find, score, and play the right word faster.
cluster.intro: The Scrabble cluster pulls together every tool and list that helps you find high-scoring plays, remember the short words that clear racks, and understand how the scoring actually works. Pick the tool or list that matches your situation.
cluster.description: Tools, lists, and strategy guides for Scrabble and Words With Friends — rack solvers, high-scoring word lists, Q-without-U words, and scoring rules.

faq.q: Which helper should I use — Scrabble or Words With Friends?
faq.a: Use the one that matches the game you are playing — tile values differ enough that the high-score play is often different.

faq.q: Are all these words tournament-legal?
faq.a: Lists use the public-domain ENABLE1 word list — close to TWL/SOWPODS but not identical. Confirm unusual words in your game's dictionary before challenging.

faq.q: Where do I start if I'm new?
faq.a: Memorise the 2-letter list, learn how the blank tile works, then read the scoring guide. Those three unlock most intermediate plays.

tools.title: Scrabble Word Finder — Kefiw

current.description: Find the highest-scoring Scrabble words you can play with your tiles.
current.intro: Enter your rack (up to 7 letters, use ? for blanks) and get every playable Scrabble word, sorted by point value.
current.outcomeLine: Your rack plus the board — every playable word ranked by score.
howTo: Enter your tiles. Use ? for blanks.
Results are sorted by Scrabble score, highest first.
Tap any word to copy it to your clipboard.

examples.title: Rack: QUIZZES
examples.body: Top play: QUIZZES (34 points, uses all tiles for a 50-point bingo).

faq.q: Are tile values exact?
faq.a: Yes — standard tournament Scrabble tile values.

faq.q: Does this include bingo bonus?
faq.a: Yes, +50 if you use all 7 tiles.

tool_logic.input_type: rack of up to 9 letters (a-z plus `?` for blank tiles); optional single board letter the word must play through
tool_logic.output_type: ranked array of playable words with Scrabble scores, capped at 300 results, sorted by score descending then length descending then alphabetical
processing_steps: Normalize rack: lowercase, strip non-letter/non-`?` characters, truncate to 9 characters.
Normalize board letter (if provided): lowercase, first letter only.
Load the ENABLE1 word list (~172,823 words) on first use via web worker.
Filter dictionary to words between 2 and 15 letters.
For each candidate word: if a board letter is specified, require the word to contain it; remove one instance before rack-fit check.
Check rack fit with `canMakeFromRack` — blanks (`?`) satisfy any missing letter.
Score each playable word with `wordScore` using SCRABBLE_VALUES (standard tournament tile values).
Add a +50 bingo bonus when the word uses exactly 7 tiles from the rack (i.e. `remainder.length === 7` after optional board-letter removal).
Sort by score descending, then length descending, then alphabetically.
Truncate to 300 results before returning.
Client re-sorts to alphabetical in Basic mode; keeps score order in Scored mode.
data_sources: ENABLE1 public-domain English word list (~172,823 entries, `public/data/enable.txt`), loaded on first use via web worker.
SCRABBLE_VALUES tile map in `src/lib/dict.ts` (Q/Z=10, J/X=8, K=5, F/H/V/W/Y=4, B/C/M/P=3, D/G=2, A/E/I/L/N/O/R/S/T/U=1).
tool_logic.fallback_behavior: Empty rack returns no results. No phonetic, morphological, or proper-noun fallback. Reset button clears rack and board letter together.
known_limitations: Scores blanks as full tile value. Standard Scrabble rules score a blank as 0 — this tool overstates the score by the value of each letter represented by a blank.
Bingo bonus is awarded only when the word consumes 7 rack tiles. A 7-letter word that uses a board letter plus 6 rack tiles is not treated as a bingo, matching real play.
Dictionary is ENABLE1, not TWL or SOWPODS. A handful of tournament-valid words (e.g. recent additions, SOWPODS-only entries) may not appear; a few ENABLE1 words are not TWL-legal.
No board context beyond a single "plays through" letter. Does not model double-letter, triple-letter, double-word, triple-word premium squares, adjacent letters, hooks, or open-parallel plays.
No proper nouns, abbreviations, or acronyms — ENABLE1 excludes them.
Board-letter check accepts the first occurrence only; when the same letter repeats in the word, only one instance is treated as the board tile.
Results capped at 300. Very open racks (e.g. lots of common letters) may hit the cap.

inputs: rack (single-line text input, uppercase display, font-mono, maxLength 9, autofocused)
board letter (single-character optional input, uppercase display, maxLength 1)
filters: mode: Basic | Scored — Basic sorts results alphabetically; Scored keeps the score-ranked order
outputs: Summary card: best play and its score
Stats card: total plays, second-best play, score gap, bingo candidate count, best short play (≤4 letters), best long play (≥5)
Takeaway card (conditional): bingo candidate count and +50 bonus reminder
Takeaway card (conditional): board-letter constraint notice when set
Results table: Word | Length | Score (Score column hidden in Basic mode)
Copy-all button that dumps the result list (with or without scores depending on mode)
Next-step card linking to WWF Helper and Word Unscrambler

claim_allowed: Ranks playable Scrabble words by point value using standard tournament tile values
Supports blanks (`?`) in the rack
Supports a single optional "plays through" board letter
Flags 7-letter bingo candidates and adds the +50 bonus
Runs entirely in the browser (no server round-trip)
claim_forbidden: Models the full board, premium squares, adjacent words, hooks, or parallel plays
Scores blanks as 0 (as tournament rules require)
Uses the TWL or SOWPODS tournament word list — it uses ENABLE1
Includes proper nouns, abbreviations, or acronyms
Respects recent dictionary updates beyond ENABLE1

tools._writer_note: tool_logic is verified from source code. Claim only what appears in content_guidance.claim_allowed; avoid anything in claim_forbidden.
notes_for_writer: Tool pages get a tight intro (60-100 words) + howTo + examples + FAQ.
Do NOT write long-form prose on the tool page — that belongs in the linked articles.
Expand the FAQ if it has fewer than 5 entries. Follow PAA rules.
Respect tool_logic: do not invent filters, data sources, or behaviors not present in ui_fields / processing_steps.

tools.title: Words With Friends Word Finder — Kefiw

current.description: Find the highest-scoring Words With Friends plays from your tiles.
current.intro: Enter your rack and get every valid Words With Friends play, scored using WWF tile values.
current.outcomeLine: WWF tile values and dictionary — different game, different answers.
howTo: Enter your tiles. Use ? for blanks.
Results are sorted by WWF score.

examples.title: Rack: SQUEEZE
examples.body: Top play: SQUEEZE (high-value Z and Q combo).

faq.q: Are the tile values different from Scrabble?
faq.a: Yes — Words With Friends uses its own tile distribution and values.

tool_logic.input_type: rack of up to 9 letters (a-z plus `?` for blank tiles); optional single board letter the word must play through
tool_logic.output_type: ranked array of playable words with WWF scores, capped at 300 results, sorted by score descending then length descending then alphabetical
processing_steps: Normalize rack: lowercase, strip non-letter/non-`?` characters, truncate to 9 characters.
Normalize board letter (if provided): lowercase, first letter only.
Load the ENABLE1 word list (~172,823 words) on first use via web worker.
Filter dictionary to words between 2 and 15 letters.
For each candidate word: if a board letter is specified, require it in the word; remove one instance before rack-fit check.
Check rack fit with `canMakeFromRack` — blanks (`?`) satisfy any missing letter.
Score each playable word with `wordScore` using WWF_VALUES (Words With Friends tile distribution).
Add a +35 bingo bonus (WWF standard) when the word uses exactly 7 tiles from the rack.
Sort by score descending, then length descending, then alphabetically.
Truncate to 300 results before returning.
data_sources: ENABLE1 public-domain English word list (~172,823 entries, `public/data/enable.txt`), shared with Scrabble Helper.
WWF_VALUES tile map in `src/lib/dict.ts`. Differs from Scrabble: J=10 (vs 8), B/F/H/M/P=4 (vs 3/4/4/3/3), L=2 (vs 1), N=2 (vs 1), U=2 (vs 1), Y=3 (vs 4), C=4 (vs 3), G=3 (vs 2).
tool_logic.fallback_behavior: Empty rack returns no results. No phonetic, morphological, or proper-noun fallback. Reset clears rack and board letter together.
known_limitations: Same blank-scoring caveat as Scrabble Helper: blanks score full tile value here, but real WWF scores them as 0.
Bingo bonus fires only when 7 rack tiles are consumed — a 7-letter word that uses a board letter plus 6 rack tiles is not flagged as bingo.
No board context beyond a single "plays through" letter. Does not model WWF premium squares (triple-letter, triple-word, or the different square layout), hooks, or parallel plays.

Uses the ENABLE1 dictionary as a reasonable proxy for the WWF dictionary — the two overlap heavily but are not identical. A handful of WWF-legal slang and informal words may be missing; a few ENABLE1 words may not be WWF-legal.
No proper nouns, abbreviations, or acronyms.
Board-letter check accepts the first occurrence only.
Results capped at 300.

inputs: rack (single-line text input, uppercase display, font-mono, maxLength 9, autofocused)
filters: mode: Basic | Scored — Basic sorts results alphabetically; Scored keeps the score-ranked order
outputs: Summary card: best play and its WWF score
Stats card: total plays, second-best, score gap, bingo count, best short play (≤4), best long play (≥5)
Takeaway card (conditional): bingo candidate count with +35 WWF bonus reminder
Takeaway card (conditional): board-letter constraint notice when set
Results table: Word | Length | Score (Score column hidden in Basic mode)
Copy-all button
Next-step card linking to Scrabble Helper and Word Unscrambler

claim_allowed: Ranks playable Words With Friends plays by point value using WWF tile values
Supports blanks (`?`) in the rack
Supports a single optional "plays through" board letter
Flags 7-letter bingo candidates and adds the +35 WWF bonus
Runs entirely in the browser
claim_forbidden: Uses the official Zynga Words With Friends dictionary — it uses ENABLE1 as a close proxy
Models the WWF board, premium squares, or parallel plays
Scores blanks as 0 (as real WWF scoring does)
Handles the WWF2 ruleset variants (Solo Challenge, Lightning Round) specifically
Includes proper nouns, player names, or brand names

tools.title: Word Unscrambler — Kefiw

current.description: Unscramble letters into valid English words. Fast, free, runs entirely in your browser.
current.intro: Paste a set of scrambled letters and get every valid English word you can build from them. Works offline, runs in your browser, and returns results in milliseconds.
current.outcomeLine: Paste your letters, see every real word they make — ranked by length or score.
howTo: Type or paste your scrambled letters into the input box.
Click Unscramble (or just wait — the tool runs as you type).
Results are grouped by length, longest first.
Tap any word to copy it to your clipboard.

examples.title: Input: TEINGL
examples.body: Returns: tingle, ingle, tile, tine, gent, gilt, lien, line, nite, tien, tile, gel, lie, ten…

examples.title: Input: AEILNRT
examples.body: Returns: retinal, latrine, ratline, reliant, trenail, entail, linter, rental…

faq.q: Does this support wildcards?
faq.a: Yes — use ? for a single blank tile. You can combine known letters with wildcards, e.g. "ca?t".

faq.q: What dictionary is used?
faq.a: An open-source English word list covering common Scrabble- and Words With Friends-valid words.

faq.q: Does it work offline?
faq.a: Yes. All processing runs locally in your browser. No server calls.

tool_logic.input_type: string of letters (a-z, plus `?` for blank/wildcard tiles)
tool_logic.output_type: array of every dictionary word that can be spelled from the input letters, sorted by length descending then alphabetical
processing_steps: Load the chosen word list on first use via web worker: Game list = ENABLE1 (~172,823 words) or Full list = dict.txt (~370,105 words).
Normalize input: lowercase, strip non-letter/non-`?` characters.
For each dictionary word within the min/max length range: use `canMakeFromRack` to check whether it can be built from the input letters (blanks satisfy any missing letter).
Sort matches by length descending, then alphabetically.
Fallback: if minLen > 2 produces zero matches, re-run with minLen = 2 and surface an amber "No words found at N+ letters" notice.
Client optionally computes per-word Scrabble and WWF scores when the "Show scores" checkbox is enabled.
data_sources: ENABLE1 word list (`public/data/enable.txt`, ~172,823 entries) — the "Game list" mode used for casual Scrabble/WWF play.
Broader English dictionary (`public/data/dict.txt`, ~370,105 entries) — the "Full list" mode that includes archaic, technical, and inflected forms.
SCRABBLE_VALUES and WWF_VALUES for optional score display.
tool_logic.fallback_behavior: Empty input returns no results. If minLen > 2 yields nothing, automatically retries at minLen = 2 and displays a fallback banner. No phonetic or transliteration fallback.
known_limitations: Requires every letter of the candidate word to be spellable from the input (blanks cover gaps). Does not return anagrams that need extra letters.
No result cap — very open letter sets ("aeinorst") can produce thousands of matches and slow the render.
Full list includes archaic, technical, and inflected forms; some are not valid Scrabble/WWF plays.
Neither list includes proper nouns, abbreviations, or acronyms.
Wildcard handling is length-only — `?` represents one blank tile, not a multi-character wildcard.
No pattern or positional matching — use Word Finder or Pattern Solver for those.
Scores, when shown, follow the same blank-scoring overcount as the rack helpers (blanks scored at full tile value).

filters: show scores: toggle for inline Scrabble + WWF scores
outputs: Amber fallback banner when min-length fallback fires
Summary card: total word count
Stats card: longest word, shortest word, best Scrabble play with score, best WWF play with score
Comparison card: count by word length (top 4 lengths)
Takeaway: best short play (≤4 letters) with Scrabble score
Result list grouped by length (or flat when scores are shown)
Copy button per result group
Next-step card linking to Scrabble Helper and WWF Helper

claim_allowed: Finds every valid English word that can be spelled from the input letters
Supports blank tiles (`?`)
Offers a compact game list (ENABLE1) and a broader dictionary (dict.txt)
Optionally shows Scrabble and WWF scores inline
Runs entirely in the browser
claim_forbidden: Finds only full-letter anagrams (use Anagram Solver for that)
Supports multi-letter wildcards or positional patterns (use Word Finder / Pattern Solver)
Includes proper nouns, slang, or acronyms

tools.title: Word Finder by Letters — Kefiw

current.description: Find words that can be made from a specific set of letters, with optional minimum and maximum length filters.
current.intro: Enter the letters you have (including blanks as ?) and filter by word length. Useful for Scrabble, Words With Friends, and general word puzzles. For prefix, suffix, or substring searches, use the dedicated Words Starting With, Words Ending With, or Words Containing tools.
current.outcomeLine: Pattern-match with ? and * across the whole dictionary.
howTo: Optionally set a minimum and maximum word length.
Tap any result to copy it.

examples.title: Input: RSTLNE?
examples.body: Returns ELDEST, LETTERS, RELENTS, STERN…

faq.q: How many blanks can I use?
faq.a: Up to 2 — same as real Scrabble.

tool_logic.input_type: letters (a-z plus `?` for blanks) OR a fixed-length pattern (a-z plus `?`/`_`/`.` as wildcards)
tool_logic.output_type: array of dictionary words matching either the letter rack (with min/max length filters) or the positional pattern
processing_steps: Load the chosen word list on first use: Game list = ENABLE1 or Full list = dict.txt.
If search mode is "Letters": strip whitespace, call the `unscramble` worker with minLen/maxLen — same rack-fit logic as Word Unscrambler but with separate length bounds.

If search mode is "Pattern": pattern length fixes the candidate word length. For each dictionary word of that length, call `matchesPattern` — any position whose pattern char is `?`, `_`, or `.` accepts any letter; other positions must match exactly (case-insensitive).
Letters mode sorts by length descending then alphabetically; Pattern mode returns in the source word-list order (effectively alphabetical from the dictionary file).
Optional Scrabble + WWF score display when toggled.
Lightweight variants (`5-letter-word-finder`, `6-letter-word-finder`, `7-letter-word-finder`) lock the pattern length to 5/6/7 and hide the min/max controls.
tool_logic.fallback_behavior: Empty input returns no results. No automatic min-length fallback (unlike Word Unscrambler). No phonetic fallback.
known_limitations: Pattern mode matches exact-length candidates only — a 4-character pattern cannot match a 5-letter word.
Pattern wildcards (`?`, `_`, `.`) are single-character only — no regex quantifiers, no character classes.
Letters mode does not support a "must include these letters" constraint — use Words Containing for substring matching instead.
No position-aware scoring, no premium-square modelling.
Neither word list includes proper nouns, abbreviations, or acronyms.
Does not deduplicate between the two word lists — switching mode refetches and rebuilds the index.

inputs: letters or pattern (single-line text input, font-mono, autofocused; maxLength is locked on the 5/6/7-letter variants)
min length: dropdown 2–8 (letters mode only; hidden on locked-length variants)
max length: dropdown 5–15 (letters mode only; hidden on locked-length variants)
filters: word list: Game list (ENABLE1) | Full list (dict.txt)
show scores: toggle for inline Scrabble + WWF scores
outputs: Summary card: match count, with the pattern echoed when in Pattern mode
Stats card: mode, longest, shortest, best Scrabble, best WWF; plus wildcard and fixed-char counts in Pattern mode
Comparison card: count by word length (top 4 lengths)
Takeaway (conditional): most common result length
Result list — grouped by length when scores are off, flat when scores are on
Next-step card linking to Words Starting With / Ending With / Containing

claim_allowed: Two search modes: Letters (anagram-style with min/max length) and Pattern (positional, fixed length)
Accepts single-letter wildcards (`?`, `_`, `.`) in Pattern mode
Supports blanks (`?`) in Letters mode
Offers a compact game list (ENABLE1) and a broader dictionary (dict.txt)
Runs entirely in the browser
Powers the 5-, 6-, and 7-letter variants with the pattern length locked
claim_forbidden: Prefix/suffix/substring search — those are separate tools
Scrabble/WWF board modelling, premium squares, or hook plays

articles.title: Best 2-Letter Scrabble Words — Ranked by Score and Use
articles.subhead: Which two-letter plays actually matter, and why.

current.description: The highest-value 2-letter Scrabble words ranked by score and playability, with the ones most worth memorising first.
current.outcomeLine: Memorise this shortlist and your hook-play score goes up immediately.
current.intro: Two-letter words are the backbone of Scrabble scoring. Every hook play and parallel extension uses one. Learn the ones that score best and crop up most often.
keyPoints: QI and ZA are the single biggest payoff: 11 and 11 points on a bare board, much more on bonus squares.
JO is the third-highest: 9 points, and it lets you dump a J without a friendly vowel position.
Focus memorisation on words that combine a high-value letter (Q, Z, J, X) with a common vowel — those are the ones you actually draw.
Plural-adding twos like AS, ES, IS, OS combined with a final S give you parallel-play flexibility.
Vowel dumps (AA, AE, AI, OE, OI) become valuable when you have an unbalanced rack.

examples.title: QI (11)
examples.body: Eastern life-force energy. The single most valuable 2-letter word because it solves the Q-without-U problem.

examples.title: ZA (11)
examples.body: Slang for pizza. Same point value as QI but with a more common tile.

examples.title: JO (9)
examples.body: Scottish sweetheart. Gives the J somewhere to go.

examples.title: XI / XU (9)
examples.body: XI is a Greek letter; XU is a Vietnamese monetary unit. Both dump X on short plays.

whenToUse.note: Paste your rack and it surfaces every 2-letter play, sorted by score, with hook positions on the board.
Great for exploring every short word a specific rack can produce — filter to 2 letters.

faq.q: How many legal 2-letter Scrabble words are there?
faq.a: About 100 in the standard tournament dictionary (TWL); a few more in SOWPODS. Memorising the full list takes an afternoon and pays off for years.

faq.q: Are all 2-letter words playable as hooks?
faq.a: Yes — every 2-letter word is a potential hook for parallel plays. Which is why they're the highest-leverage vocabulary you can learn.

Main expansion goes into longform_markdown as 4-6 H2 sections.
Preserve every relatedId and toolId — those are internal links.

articles.title: Best 3-Letter Scrabble Words — High-Value Plays to Memorise
articles.subhead: The three-letter words that win more games than the length suggests.

current.description: The most valuable 3-letter Scrabble words — J, Q, X, Z plays plus the vowel-dumpers that save a tight rack.
current.outcomeLine: Three-letter high scorers let you unload awkward tiles without opening the board.
current.intro: Three-letter words are the workhorse length in Scrabble — long enough to hit two bonus squares, short enough to fit in tight board positions. The best ones combine a high-value tile with two easy letters.
keyPoints: ZAX (19), JEU (10), JIB (12), ZED (13), ZIG (13): the J/Z/X plays you want to memorise first.
QUA, QAT, QIS, SUQ — the Q-friendly plays that solve a locked-in Q.
Vowel-heavy 3-letter words (AAH, OOT, EEL) clear vowel-heavy racks without changing the board much.
Many 3-letter words hook onto 2-letter plays for parallel scoring — look for shared endings.
Focus first on J/Q/X/Z words, then the vowel dumpers, then the rest.

examples.title: ZAX (19)
examples.body: A slater's chopping tool. Three-letter high-scorer that also dumps the X.

examples.title: JIB (12)
examples.body: Triangular sail. Clears a J without needing specific positions.

examples.title: QAT (12)
examples.body: An East African shrub (also khat). Lets you play Q without U.

examples.title: AAH (6)
examples.body: An exclamation. A vowel-dump when your rack is AAAOOOU.

whenToUse.note: Shows every 3-letter play ordered by score, with hook positions highlighted.
Tile values differ — same 3-letter word can score very differently. Use this for WWF games.

faq.q: Why focus on 3-letter words over 4-letter?
faq.a: More 3-letter plays fit on a contested board, and they're the length where J, Q, X, and Z plays are easiest to produce.

faq.q: How do 3-letter words stack with bonus squares?
faq.a: On a triple-letter square, a 3-letter play with the X (worth 8) scores 24 from that tile alone — often beating a longer play with only 1-point tiles.

notes_for_writer: Keep the existing structured fields but tighten/expand as needed.

articles.title: Best Bingo Words in Scrabble — 7-Letter Patterns Worth Memorising
articles.subhead: The 7-letter words that actually come up — suffix-driven, vowel-balanced, high-probability.

current.description: The 7-letter words most likely to materialise on your rack, organised by stem + suffix patterns.
current.outcomeLine: Learn the common stem-plus-suffix combinations and bingo rate goes from occasional to routine.
current.intro: A bingo (using all 7 tiles for a 50-point bonus) looks like magic but is mostly pattern recognition. Most bingos come from a short stem plus a common suffix — learn the stems, and the bingo finds you.
keyPoints: Common -ING bingos: TESTING, RAISING, LEADING. Keep ING tiles when the stem letters are common.
Common -IEST bingos: EASIEST, PRICIEST, HEAVIEST. -IEST with a familiar adjective stem.
Common -IER bingos: RANGIER, PRICIER, HEAVIER. Same stems as -IEST minus one letter.
-ATION words: STATION, RELATION, DONATION. Great when you have a vowel-heavy rack with T and N.
The "SATIRE" and "RETAINS" stems (AEINRST) have the most 7-letter anagrams in the language — hundreds of bingos from that set.

examples.title: Rack: AEINRST
examples.body: Forms RETAINS, RETSINA, STAINER, STEARIN, NASTIER and many more. Known as the "bingo stem" for a reason.

examples.title: Rack: AEEIRST
examples.body: Forms AERIEST, SERIATE. One less consonant, still bingo-rich.

examples.title: Pattern: stem + ING
examples.body: Stems like TRAIL-, SAIL-, DRAIN- all take ING. If you have I, N, G plus a usable stem, scan for -ING options.

whenToUse.note: Paste your 7-tile rack — it surfaces every bingo plus the board-contact options.
For practising off the board — take the bingo stems (AEINRST, AEEIRST) and work every anagram.
Great for filtering by length 7 to study bingo-length vocabulary.

faq.q: What is the most common bingo stem?
faq.a: AEINRST — the "retains stem". It has more 7-letter anagrams than any other combination. Memorising its core words pays off repeatedly.

faq.q: How often should I expect a bingo?
faq.a: Strong club players average one bingo every 2–3 games. Learning stems and keeping rack balance are the two biggest levers.

articles.title: How to Find Bingo Plays — Rack Balance, Stems, and Board Scan
articles.subhead: The habits that let you spot a 50-point play when the board is tight.

current.description: A practical process for finding bingo plays — rack balance, stem recognition, and board-scan technique.
current.outcomeLine: Keep a balanced rack, know your stems, and scan the board for hook squares — bingos appear more often than you think.
current.intro: Finding a bingo is two problems stacked — first, having a rack that can form one; second, finding a place on the board it will fit. Good players manage both in parallel throughout the game.
keyPoints: Rack balance: aim for 3-4 vowels and 3-4 consonants. A 6-vowel or 6-consonant rack almost never bingos.
Keep stems when you exchange — AEINRST and similar have enormous bingo potential.
Scan the board for single exposed letters — these are bingo hook points. A lone T can support STATIONS, SEATING, etc.
Rearrange mentally: group vowels and consonants separately, look for -ING, -ED, -IES, -IER endings.
If bingo is not possible, the setup matters — play in a way that leaves your rack bingo-ready for next turn.

examples.title: Rack ANIRESE
examples.body: Rearrange: AEEINRS. Near the bingo stem. SEARINE? No. Scan for hooks — an existing R on the board enables RESIANE (invalid), ARENITES (8 letters, not bingo), etc. Takes trained eye.

examples.title: Hook hunt
examples.body: Board has an isolated S with space above it. Your rack forms a 7-letter word ending S — your bingo lives in that column.

examples.title: Exchange decision
examples.body: Rack QIIIUU — hopeless. Exchange 5+ tiles. Keeping Q+I only and drawing fresh usually beats forcing a low-score play.

whenToUse.note: Paste rack and board state — it finds every bingo placement, not just the word candidates.
For rack-only practice — take a random 7-letter stem and see every bingo it can form.
For targeted searches like "7-letter words ending -ING".

faq.q: Should I always play a bingo if I see one?
faq.a: Usually yes — +50 points is hard to beat. But if the bingo opens a triple-word lane for the opponent, consider the tempo tradeoff.

faq.q: What if I never find bingos?
faq.a: Two fixes: one, learn the AEINRST stem and its anagrams; two, audit your rack-balance habits. Most missed bingos are self-inflicted from poor rack management.

articles.title: Best Scrabble Words With Z — High-Scoring Plays to Memorise
articles.subhead: Where to dump the Z before a vowel problem traps you.

current.description: The most valuable Scrabble words containing Z, ranked by score and playability — 2, 3, and 4-letter options for tight board positions.
current.outcomeLine: Z is a 10-point tile — use it on short, bonus-adjacent plays and it pays for itself.
current.intro: The Z is worth 10 points — one of the two highest-value tiles in Scrabble. Short Z plays are usually more valuable than long ones because you can land them on premium squares and hook off existing words.
keyPoints: ZA (11) — slang for pizza, the single most useful 2-letter Z play.
ZO (11) — a Tibetan yak hybrid. Another 2-letter Z dump.
ZAX (19) — a slater's tool. Three-letter Z+X monster when the tiles show up together.
ZEE (12), ZIT (12), ZIP (14), ZAP (14), ZIG (13), ZED (13) — all 3-letter Z words for flexible placements.
ZEBRA, ZESTY, CRAZY, JAZZ — 5-letter plays good for bingo-setup or triple-word lanes.

examples.title: ZA on a triple-letter
examples.body: Z on TL square = 30 just from the Z tile, plus base. Often 30-40 points from a 2-letter word.

examples.title: ZAX (19 base)
examples.body: Combines Z(10) + X(8) in one play. On any bonus square it scores 40+.

examples.title: JAZZ
examples.body: Rare but real — 29 points base from the double Z plus J. If both Zs show up (there's only one blank-free Z in the bag, but second Z can be a blank), look for JAZZ placements.

whenToUse.note: Paste rack with Z — surfaces every Z play sorted by score with hook positions.
Z is 10 in WWF too, but bonus-square positions differ — use this for WWF.
For offline study — "3-letter words containing Z".

faq.q: How many Zs are in a Scrabble bag?
faq.a: One. Plus two blanks that can substitute. Holding onto a Z waiting for a perfect play is usually a mistake — play it as soon as you have a decent option.

faq.q: Is ZO really a valid word?
faq.a: Yes in TWL and SOWPODS. It refers to a Tibetan hybrid of yak and cow. Frequently disputed at kitchen-table games, consistently valid in tournaments.

articles.title: Best Scrabble Words With X — Short Plays That Punch Above Their Weight
articles.subhead: X is the second-highest scorer and the easiest to place — here is how to make it count.

current.description: The most valuable Scrabble words with X, including the critical 2-letter X plays and common 3/4-letter options.
current.outcomeLine: X is an 8-point tile that plays short. Hook it, park it on a premium square, move on.
current.intro: X scores 8 points and plays surprisingly short. The 2-letter X words are the single most-useful vocabulary list in Scrabble — they give you parallel-play bingos that use the X as a shared letter.
keyPoints: XI (9), XU (9) — the two 2-letter X words. Memorise both.
AX, EX, OX — the other 2-letter X plays. Common vowel + X combinations.
ZAX (19), LAX (10), LOX (10), OXY (13), HEX (13), POX (12), TAX (10), VEX (13) — 3-letter X plays for bonus squares.
X is also a "hook letter" — add it to existing plays to form new words parallel to them.
X on a triple-letter square = 24 from the X alone. On a double-word with AX = 20+. Easy bonuses.

examples.title: AX parallel play
examples.body: Existing word ...CAR on board. Play AXE below to form CAR+A, AR+X (if valid), R+E. Multiple words one move.

examples.title: XI on triple-letter
examples.body: XI = X(8×3)+I(1) = 25 points from a 2-letter play. Plus any parallel scoring.

examples.title: OXY
examples.body: 3-letter OXY on a double-word: (1+8+4) × 2 = 26 points. Small play, strong payoff.

whenToUse.note: Paste rack with X — sorts all X plays by score with bonus-square awareness.
For "3-letter words with X" style offline study.

faq.q: Why is X easier to play than Q?
faq.a: X pairs with any vowel (AX, EX, OX) and several consonants. Q almost always needs a U or specific Q-without-U words. X is the more flexible high-value tile.

faq.q: How many Xs are in the bag?
faq.a: One X, plus blanks. Unlike Z, X shows up often enough that hoarding it is rarely worth it — play it when you have a usable spot.

articles.title: Best Scrabble Words With J — Eight Points, Awkward Placement
articles.subhead: J is valuable but stubborn — here are the short plays that solve awkward-rack situations.

current.description: The most useful Scrabble words with J — short plays and common extensions that prevent the J from stranding on your rack.
current.outcomeLine: J needs a vowel to land softly — know JO, JAB, JET, JIB and the rest and you unload J smoothly.
current.intro: J is worth 8 points but often feels stuck on your rack. Unlike X and Z, there is no 2-letter J word — so the shortest plays are 3-letter. Knowing the J-friendly triples turns an awkward tile into a productive play.
keyPoints: No 2-letter J words exist — JO was removed and has not returned.
3-letter J words: JO(?) no — JAB(12), JAG(11), JAM(12), JAR(10), JAW(13), JAY(13), JET(10), JEU(10), JIB(12), JIG(11), JOB(12), JOT(10), JOY(13), JUG(11), JUS(10), JUT(10).
4-letter dumps: JAZZ, JOKE, JUMP, JEEP, JOWL.
J on a triple-letter square = 24 from the J alone. 3-letter J plays on bonus squares score 30-40.
If you have no vowel, exchanging is almost always better than holding a J indefinitely.

examples.title: JAW on a double-letter
examples.body: J(8) + A(1×2) + W(4) = 14 points base, plus any parallel. 3-letter play, double-digit score.

examples.title: JEU / JIB / JOE dumps
examples.body: When no hook exists, these short J words let you play J without a huge setup.

examples.title: JAZZ
examples.body: With a blank filling for the second Z, JAZZ is a 29-point play. Rare rack, big payoff.

whenToUse.note: Finds every J play on your rack + board, sorted by score.
For offline study — "3-letter words with J".

faq.q: Why is there no 2-letter J word?
faq.a: Historical accident of the dictionaries. JO used to be valid in some older lists but is not in current TWL. Every legal 2-letter word list is worth checking against a current source.

faq.q: Should I hold a J for a perfect play?
faq.a: Usually no. Each turn you hold a J, you are playing with a 6-tile rack effectively. Play it at the next decent opportunity — perfection is rare.

articles.title: How Words With Friends Scoring Differs From Scrabble
articles.h1: How Words With Friends Scoring Differs
articles.subhead: Same grid feeling, different tile values and bonus squares.

current.description: The concrete scoring differences between Scrabble and Words With Friends — tile values, bonus squares, bingo bonus, and dictionary.
current.outcomeLine: Know the tile-value and bonus-layout differences and you stop mis-costing your plays.
current.intro: Words With Friends looks like Scrabble at a glance, but the tile values, bonus-square positions, and dictionary all differ. Plays that feel strong in one game can be mediocre in the other.
keyPoints: Tile values differ: X is 8 in Scrabble but 9 in WWF. J is 8 in Scrabble but 10 in WWF. Q and Z are 10 in both.
Bonus-square positions are different — triple-letter squares sit where Scrabble has double-letter, and vice versa.
Bingo bonus (use all 7 tiles) = 50 points in Scrabble, 35 in WWF.
Dictionaries differ: WWF uses a modified ENABLE list; Scrabble uses TWL or SOWPODS. Some words valid in one fail in the other.
Net effect: the same word can score meaningfully differently. A rack full of letter-specific plays deserves a re-check when switching games.

examples.title: JUMBO
examples.body: Scrabble: 14 base. WWF: 16 base (J is 10). Same letters, different value.

examples.title: Bingo
examples.body: Full-rack play is +50 in Scrabble, +35 in WWF. The bingo threshold worth chasing is lower in WWF.

whenToUse.note: Uses WWF tile values and dictionary — not interchangeable with the Scrabble helper.
Use this ONLY for Scrabble play. Tile values are different.

faq.q: Is one game easier than the other?
faq.a: WWF's lower bingo bonus and shifted board make it a slightly lower-variance game. Scrabble rewards big high-value plays more.

faq.q: Can I use a Scrabble solver for WWF?
faq.a: No — tile values and dictionary both differ. A Scrabble solver will score your rack wrongly and may suggest invalid words.

articles.title: Q Without U in Scrabble — Every Word Explained — Kefiw
articles.subhead: The complete list of Q-words that do not need a U, and why they exist

current.description: Why some Q-words do not need a U, which ones to memorise, and how to use them for emergency Scrabble plays.
current.intro: Drawing a Q without a U is the classic Scrabble nightmare. This guide lists every legal Q-without-U word and explains why they exist.

faq.q: Is QI really a word?
faq.a: Yes. QI is the Chinese concept of life energy and has been legal in Scrabble for decades. It is one of the highest-scoring 2-letter plays.

faq.q: What about QATS and QOPHS?
faq.a: Both are valid plurals of QAT (a plant chewed as a stimulant) and QOPH (a Hebrew letter).

faq.q: Can I keep waiting for a U?
faq.a: Only up to a point. If you are holding Q for more than two or three turns with no U in the bag, play a Q-no-U word to clear the tile.

articles.title: How to Use Scrabble Blanks — Strategy Guide — Kefiw
articles.subhead: When to hold, when to play, and how to maximise the zero-point tile

current.description: Blank tiles score zero but can represent any letter. This guide covers when to play them for bingos, when to hold, and how to find the highest-value blank play.
current.intro: Blanks are the most valuable tiles in Scrabble despite scoring zero points themselves. This guide explains the strategy around them.

faq.q: How much is a blank worth?
faq.a: Zero points on its own — but it often enables 50-100 point plays via bingos and word-multipliers.

faq.q: Should I always hold blanks for a bingo?
faq.a: Usually yes — the 50-point bonus for playing all 7 tiles is nearly always worth one or two turns of patience.

faq.q: Can a blank be challenged?
faq.a: No. Blanks are legally any letter the player declares at time of placement.

articles.title: Scrabble Scoring Explained — Tile Values, Bonus Squares, Bingos — Kefiw
articles.subhead: Tile values, bonus squares, multipliers, and the bingo bonus

current.description: How Scrabble scoring works, including tile values, double/triple letter squares, double/triple word squares, and the 50-point bingo bonus.
current.intro: Scrabble scoring looks simple but has a few subtleties. Here is the full reference — tile values, bonus squares, and the 50-point bingo bonus.

faq.q: Does the bonus square affect cross-words?
faq.a: Yes. Any bonus square you cover affects every word that uses a letter on that square.

faq.q: Do letter bonuses multiply before or after word bonuses?
faq.a: Letter bonuses are applied first, then the whole word score is multiplied by any word-multiplier covered.

faq.q: What is the maximum single-play score?
faq.a: OXYPHENBUTAZONE has a theoretical max of 1,778 points in a contrived but legal setup.

current.intro: A bingo is worth 50 points on top of the word score — the single biggest edge in Scrabble. This guide covers the habits and patterns that make bingos reliable.

faq.q: How often should I expect a bingo?
faq.a: Top club-level players average one bingo every 3-4 turns. Beginners average roughly zero. The gap is the biggest skill-rating lever.

faq.q: Do bingos have to be 7 letters exactly?
faq.a: Yes. Playing 8 letters (with a board-hook) also earns the bonus but is much rarer.

faq.q: Why are some Q words exempt from needing a U?

faq.q: Is "QWERTY" really valid?

faq.q: What about words with Q followed by a non-U vowel?
faq.a: A few exist: QINTAR, QINDARKA are examples where Q is followed by I.

enhancement_brief.goal: Lift each article from short-form summary to authority-grade long-form. Target 1,200+ words per article while keeping the structured fields crisp.
per_article_outputs: 1. Fill `enhanced` with any structured-field overrides (usually: tighter intro, expanded keyPoints, more examples, richer FAQ).
2. Fill `longform_markdown` with 800-1200 word prose body (markdown, H2 sections only — no H1).
3. Do not modify `current` — that is the baseline snapshot for diffing.

rules: Each question must read like a real search query — not marketing-speak.
First sentence of answer is a complete standalone 15-25 word definition/answer (Google quotes it directly).
Full answer 40-60 words. Tight. Front-load the answer.
Cover distinct intents: definition, comparison, edge-case, how-to, troubleshooting. Not 3 rewordings of the same angle.
Include one target keyword in at least half the questions.
Add a faq_intent tag per entry: "definition" | "comparison" | "edge-case" | "how-to" | "troubleshooting" | "cost" | "trust"
faq_rules.target_count: 5-7 entries per article, 4-6 per tool page

structure: ## H2 section 1 — deeper "what is it" / definition with context
## H2 section 2 — the mechanic / how it works (with one small example)
## H2 section 3 — when to use vs when to avoid
## H2 section 4 — worked example or walkthrough
## H2 section 6 — tool-specific tips (link out to the tool)
longform_rules.tone: Concrete, useful, first-person-plural light. No filler. No "in conclusion". Short paragraphs.
links: Every time you mention a sibling tool in this cluster, link it with the exact URL from this brief (tools[].url).
Every time you mention a sibling article, link it with the exact URL from this brief (articles[].url).
Do not invent URLs or link to external competitors.
forbidden: Medical/legal/financial advice language ("you should", "we recommend") — use neutral framing.
Generic SEO boilerplate ("in today's fast-paced world").

internal_linking.rule: Use only URLs that appear in this JSON. Prefer linking to sibling tools/articles in THIS cluster over outbound links.

quality_gates: No fabricated facts — if uncertain, phrase as "in practice" or remove.
Every H2 section earns its space — cut if it restates the intro.
Zero AI-tells: "it is important to note", "in conclusion", "let us delve", "furthermore".
